html:
HB Technology (078150.KQ) Thesis

HB Technology is not the probe-card story in the prior draft. The cleaner, more interesting thesis is a Korean inspection and repair equipment maker whose old identity was display AOI, but whose new optionality is precision inspection for IT OLED, batteries, and semiconductor glass substrates.

At KRW 3,825, the market cap is about KRW 355 billion, using roughly 92.7 million listed shares. The latest screen price is from K5’s delayed quote, and Q1 2026 was just filed: revenue KRW 36.3 billion, operating profit KRW 0.5 billion, and net income KRW 18.2 billion, per the May 15 filing summary.

The stock looks confusing because the reported P&L mixes three things: a lumpy equipment business, lower-margin BLU materials, and a large investment asset book. That complexity is the opportunity. FY2025 revenue was KRW 160.3 billion, operating income only KRW 4.4 billion, but net income was KRW 28.4 billion due to investment and equity gains. The balance sheet ended 2025 with KRW 31.1 billion cash and short-term investments, KRW 47.7 billion debt, and KRW 184.6 billion long-term investments. On a simple stub basis, the market is paying roughly KRW 185 billion for the operating business after crediting cash, debt, and investment assets. That is not expensive if the operating business is entering a capex replacement cycle.

The core variant perception is that HB Technology should not be valued as a stale LCD/OLED equipment vendor. Its real asset is machine vision, defect classification, and laser repair on large, high-value glass or panel surfaces. That skill set used to serve Samsung Display and BOE OLED fabs. It now maps directly into two better markets: 8.6-generation IT OLED and semiconductor glass-core substrates.

The glass substrate angle is the torque. Intel has said glass substrates are needed for next-generation advanced packaging later this decade, especially for AI and data-center packages. SKC is raising KRW 1.16 trillion, with a large portion earmarked for its Absolics glass substrate business. The important company-specific fact is that HB Technology was reported by TheElec to be supplying Absolics with AOI and repair equipment, including 2-micrometer and 10-micrometer pattern inspection systems plus laser repair tools. If glass substrate moves from pilot to qualification to production, HB is not selling a commodity part. It is selling yield-enabling inspection equipment into a new process where defect detection and repair determine whether the substrate line works at all.

The OLED cycle also matters. Samsung Display’s 8.6G IT OLED ramp and BOE’s B16 project create another equipment window. HB has already been named among Korean suppliers for BOE’s 8.6G OLED line. This is not a straight-line growth business, but large equipment orders can drop through with high incremental margins. The Q4 2025 quarter showed that operating leverage: KRW 51.8 billion revenue produced KRW 9.4 billion operating profit. Q1 2026 fell back to KRW 0.5 billion operating profit, but the year-over-year improvement from a Q1 2025 operating loss shows the trough is passing.

The retail context cuts both ways. HB Technology is no longer totally ignored in Korea after the May 6 glass substrate theme rally, when the stock hit limit-up alongside SKC and other glass substrate names. KRX then designated the stock as an investment warning name effective May 12. That means near-term holders include theme money, not just fundamental investors. But the pullback to KRW 3,825 gives the thesis a more reasonable entry: the stock is still only modestly above tangible book, while the operating stub is valued as if glass substrate contributes little.

The bear case is not bankruptcy. It is disappointment. Glass substrate adoption slips into 2028-2029, OLED equipment orders are delayed, battery inspection remains small, and investment assets are marked down. In that case, the market stops giving credit for optionality and values the company around 0.8x pro forma book. Bear target: KRW 2,600, down 32%.

The base case is that 2026 is a bridge year and 2027 shows normalized revenue of KRW 220-240 billion, with better mix from OLED equipment, semiconductor substrate inspection, and repair tools. EBITDA can reach KRW 40-45 billion if gross margins recover into the low-to-mid 20s and SG&A stays controlled. Applying 9x EBITDA to the operating business, then adding discounted net financial and investment assets, supports an equity value near KRW 575 billion. Base target: KRW 6,200, up 62%.

The bull case is that Absolics and other glass substrate supply chains move faster, and HB becomes one of the first proven inspection and repair vendors in a new advanced packaging process. Add a stronger 8.6G OLED capex cycle and the operating business can plausibly earn KRW 70-75 billion EBITDA in a peak year. At 10x EBITDA plus the investment asset book, the equity value can approach KRW 975 billion. Bull target: KRW 10,500, up 175%.

Current price: KRW 3,825. Bear/base/bull: KRW 2,600 / KRW 6,200 / KRW 10,500.
